card.intuition: Maintain a contiguous subarray (window) that expands and contracts as you scan left-to-right, reusing work from the previous position instead of recomputing from scratch.
card.analogy: Looking through a moving train window: instead of describing a new scene from scratch each second, you drop what just left the frame and add what just entered.
card.time_complexity: O(n) — each element enters and leaves the window at most once
card.space_complexity: O(1) for fixed-size windows; O(k) for variable windows tracking a set/map
when_to_use: Maximum or minimum sum/product of a subarray of size k
Longest substring satisfying a property (no repeats, at most k distinct chars, etc.)
Smallest contiguous subarray with sum ≥ target
Any problem asking about contiguous subarrays where brute force is O(n²)
gotchas: Fixed-size windows expand by one, shrink by one — easy. Variable windows require a condition to decide when to shrink
Track what's inside the window carefully — a frequency map or running sum prevents O(n) recomputation on each slide
Off-by-one: the window [lo, hi] is inclusive on both ends; length = hi - lo + 1

exercise.prompt: Complete the fixed-size sliding window to find the maximum sum of any subarray of length k.
exercise.starter_code:
def max_subarray_sum(arr, k):
    n = len(arr)
    if n < k:
        return None
    # Build initial window
    window_sum = sum(arr[:k])
    max_sum = window_sum
    # Slide: add new element on right, drop old element on left
    for i in range(k, n):
        window_sum += ___________
        window_sum -= ___________
        max_sum = max(max_sum, window_sum)
    return max_sum
exercise.solution:
window_sum += arr[i]         # new element entering window on the right
window_sum -= arr[i - k]     # old element leaving window on the left
hints: The new element entering is arr[i] — the rightmost element of the current window
The element leaving is arr[i - k] — exactly k positions behind the new element
exercise.test_code:
assert max_subarray_sum([2,1,5,1,3,2], 3) == 9,  'Window [5,1,3] sums to 9'
assert max_subarray_sum([1,2,3,4,5], 2)    == 9,  'Window [4,5] sums to 9'
assert max_subarray_sum([1,1,1,1], 4)      == 4,  'Only one window of size 4'
assert max_subarray_sum([3], 1)             == 3,  'Single element'
assert max_subarray_sum([1,2], 3)          is None, 'k > n returns None'
print('PASS')
